topic: Part 4 opening italic transition paragraph (EXP-116 integration)
change_summary:
EXP-116 added a one-sentence italic transition at the top of Part 4 (between

and the original

). The transition frames Part 4 as testing whether the dome's 'aggregate framework' compensates for individual-claim failures documented in Part 3.
our_argument_summary: The new Part 4 intro reads: 'Part 3 showed that individual claims fail under scrutiny. This part tests whether the model’s aggregate framework—its statistical convergence dashboard and site architecture—compensates for those individual weaknesses.' It is the connective tissue linking Part 3 (point-by-point WIN review) to Part 4 (Live Power Dashboard & Site Page Analysis), and it complements the broader roadmap box already inserted at the top of Part 3.

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: N/A — this review targets prose framing, not code.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: N/A — prose/roadmap item, not a WIN tied to monitor.py logic.

holes_found.description: 'Part 3 showed that individual claims fail under scrutiny' collapses heterogeneous verdict categories into a single rhetorical bucket. Of 70 WINs in Part 3: 14 are Self-Contradicted, 22 Misleading, 17 'Std Model Explains', 9 Refuted by Data, 4 Not Demonstrated, 4 Unfalsifiable. 'Std Model Explains' (17/70 = 24%) is not a straightforward 'failure under scrutiny' — in those cases the phenomenon is real and the dome’s numerical match is correct; the problem is that standard physics already predicted the same result. A dome defender will (correctly) flag this as a framing sleight: 'You’re collapsing "we match the data" into "we failed under scrutiny."' Either generalize to something like 'Part 3 showed that individual claims do not, in aggregate, survive as independent dome confirmations' or explicitly mention the mixed verdict pattern.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace 'individual claims fail under scrutiny' with something more precise such as: 'Part 3 evaluated every WIN individually and found that none survive as an independent dome-only confirmation — the verdicts split between self-contradiction, refutation, standard-physics explanation, and unfalsifiable framing.' This preserves brevity but defuses the 'you’re calling Std-Model-Explains a "failure"' objection.

holes_found.description: The intro undersells Part 4’s independent force. The strongest Part 4 argument — '14 of 20 claimed domains share the same fitted constant (λ_g = 8,619 km)' — is an *independent* refutation of the dashboard regardless of whether any individual WIN in Part 3 held. By framing Part 4 solely as 'whether the aggregate framework compensates for individual weaknesses,' the transition makes Part 4 look derivative of Part 3, when in fact Part 4’s shared-constant finding stands alone. A dome defender who successfully rehabilitates a few Part 3 WINs would still face Part 4’s independent collapse; the current phrasing obscures that.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a second clause: 'This part tests whether the model’s aggregate framework … compensates for those individual weaknesses — *and, independently, whether the framework’s own statistical claim (20 converging domains) holds up when the shared inputs are audited.*' Or split into two sentences.

holes_found.description: Mild redundancy with the Part 3 roadmap box inserted by the same EXP. The Part 3 box already says Part 4 'steps back to the meta-framework — the "Live Power" dashboard, statistical convergence claims, and site-page architecture — to test whether the ensemble is stronger than its parts.' The Part 4 italic intro restates essentially the same frame in different words. Readers moving top-down will see the same framing twice within 1–2 screens of scrolling.
holes_found.recommendation: Defensible as-is — a reader who deep-links to Part 4 will not have seen the Part 3 box, so some redundancy is warranted for navigation. Optional tightening: the Part 4 italic could say something slightly different (e.g., emphasize the *independent* aspect suggested above) so it adds new signal rather than paraphrasing the Part 3 box.

holes_found.description: 'Site architecture' is a slight mismatch with what Part 4 actually reviews. The section heading is 'Live Power Dashboard & Site Page Analysis'; the body analyzes specific pages (Live Power, Kill-Shot, Audit Walkthrough, Tracking) as individual evidentiary artifacts, not the site’s structural architecture. A pedantic defender could object: 'You’re not reviewing my architecture, you’re reviewing four pages.'
holes_found.recommendation: Change 'site architecture' to 'site-page architecture' (matching the Part 3 roadmap wording) or 'evidential site pages' to keep the language self-consistent across the two insertions. Trivial.

holes_found.description: The transition is italicized in grey (#555) with a bottom-margin pull-up, which matches the visual pattern later introduced in Part 5. Verify this styling is CSS-class-based rather than inline-styled long-term; inline styles make global theme changes painful. (Not a content issue; flag for integrity.)
holes_found.recommendation: Migrate both italic transitions (Part 4 and Part 5) to a shared CSS class (e.g., '.section-transition') in a future cleanup. Out of scope for this EXP.

stronger_arguments.description: The transition could do double duty by briefly signalling the evidence hierarchy that Part 2 has already discharged: 'Part 2 demonstrated internal geometric contradictions; Part 3 tested the individual claims against published data; this part now tests the aggregate framework the dome relies on when any single claim is challenged.' That makes Part 4 look like the answer to the dome’s standard escape move — 'sure, any single WIN might look weak, but 20 convergent domains can’t all be wrong' — which is in fact the steel-manned dome position the section is rebutting.
sources: dome site index.html (Live Power dashboard framing)

kernel_of_truth.description: The analyst made a deliberate choice to keep the Part 4 transition brief and narrative rather than analytical, leaning on the Part 3 roadmap box to do the heavier framing work. That choice is reasonable for reader flow and for readers who read Parts 3→4 in sequence. Adding a single italic line is low-risk, high-navigability.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Brevity is fine; imprecision is not. 'Individual claims fail under scrutiny' is the one phrase a hostile reader will quote back as evidence that the review editorializes ('you just asserted they failed'). Replacing it with verdict-aware language costs nothing and closes that opening.

text_fingerprint.transition_text: Part 3 showed that individual claims fail under scrutiny. This part tests whether the model's aggregate framework—its statistical convergence dashboard and site architecture—compensates for those individual weaknesses.
text_fingerprint.location:
data/sections.json part4 html, first

after

deeper_analysis:
Thinking adversarially as a dome defender: the paragraph hands me exactly one rhetorical opening — the word 'fail'. I would write a rebuttal that says: 'The review’s own summary table shows 17 of 70 WINs labelled "Standard Model Explains." In those cases the dome’s numerical prediction matched the measurement. That is not "failure under scrutiny" — that is the dome’s prediction being corroborated; the reviewer merely disputes credit.' This rebuttal is partly wrong (the dome’s uniqueness claim does fail in those cases), but it lets a defender spend 200 words re-litigating the definition of 'failure' instead of engaging with the structural argument Part 4 makes. Tightening the wording here removes that rhetorical foothold for negligible cost.

Separately, considering the other side of the user’s preference instruction: is there a scenario where the current phrasing is genuinely better? Yes — for non-specialist readers, 'individual claims fail under scrutiny' is a clear, skimmable signal that Part 3 reached a negative overall conclusion. More precise language ('do not, in aggregate, survive as independent dome-only confirmations') is more accurate but less immediately legible. A reasonable compromise: keep the skimmable framing but add a half-clause acknowledging mixed verdicts, e.g. 'Part 3 showed that individual claims do not, on their own terms, survive scrutiny — whether refuted, self-contradicted, or already explained by standard physics.'

No citations to verify. No factual / numerical / dome-code claims in the transition. The risk surface here is entirely rhetorical framing.
verdict_summary: Content is accurate in substance and appropriate for a brief navigational transition. Two moderate framing issues ('fail under scrutiny' collapses verdict categories; 'compensates for weaknesses' undersells Part 4’s independent refutation) argue for a minor edit but nothing structural. Integrity is sound; a dome defender cannot land a serious blow against this paragraph.
